title: Citrix NetScaler CVE-2026-3055 (CVSS 9.3): SAML IDP Out-of-Bounds Memory Read Actively Exploited via Crafted SAMLRequest — Appliance Memory Leaked Through NSC_TASS Cookie Exposing Session Tokens and Credentials

timeline.event: Citrix publishes security bulletin CTX696300 disclosing CVE-2026-3055 (CVSS 9.3) out-of-bounds read in NetScaler ADC and Gateway; patches released same day
Rapid7 and Help Net Security publish analyses warning of high exploitation likelihood; watchTowr begins technical investigation of memory overread mechanics

watchTowr publishes Part 1 technical analysis; SOCRadar documents the NSC_TASS cookie memory leak vector; Cloud Software Group releases Global Deny List signatures for version 14.1-60.52 and 14.1-60.57

watchTowr confirms in-the-wild exploitation beginning from known threat actor source IPs based on honeypot data; watchTowr publishes Part 2 revealing two distinct memory overread bugs affecting /saml/login and /wsfed/passive endpoints; active reconnaissance traffic surges

Defused researchers confirm active exploitation on X; BleepingComputer and multiple outlets report full active exploitation confirmed; attackers also probing /cgi/GetAuthMethods for authentication flow enumeration

simple_summary.what_happened: Citrix disclosed a critical vulnerability in its widely-deployed NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway products that allows attackers to leak sensitive memory from the appliance without any authentication. The flaw exists specifically in the SAML Identity Provider (SAML IDP) configuration — a common setup where NetScaler handles single sign-on authentication for enterprise applications. By sending a malformed SAML authentication request with a key field deliberately omitted, an attacker can trigger an out-of-bounds read in the appliance's memory. The leaked memory content is returned to the attacker embedded in a cookie called NSC_TASS. This memory can contain session tokens, private keys, credentials, or other sensitive data from the device's RAM. Exploitation began just four days after public disclosure.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running NetScaler ADC or NetScaler Gateway on-premises as a SAML Identity Provider (SAML IDP) on versions 14.1 before 14.1-66.59, 13.1 before 13.1-62.23, or FIPS/NDcPP variants. This specifically affects deployments where NetScaler is configured using the 'add authentication samlIdPProfile' command. NetScaler products are widely deployed in enterprise environments as application delivery controllers and VPN gateways, particularly in financial services, healthcare, and government. Cloud-managed Citrix instances are not affected.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: The attacker identifies target organizations using internet-exposed NetScaler ADC or Gateway appliances configured as SAML IDPs by probing the /cgi/GetAuthMethods endpoint, which reveals enabled authentication methods including SAML IDP. Step 2: The attacker crafts a malformed SAMLRequest payload sent to the /saml/login endpoint that deliberately omits the AssertionConsumerServiceURL field, which is a required field the NetScaler code fails to properly validate before using. Step 3: The NetScaler appliance processes the malformed request and due to insufficient input validation performs an out-of-bounds read — reading memory beyond the intended buffer boundaries in the SAML processing code. Step 4: The leaked memory contents are encoded and returned to the attacker embedded in the NSC_TASS response cookie — up to several hundred bytes of raw appliance memory per request, which the attacker can read by parsing the cookie value. Step 5: By sending repeated requests the attacker can harvest multiple memory regions, potentially extracting session tokens for authenticated administrator sessions, private TLS certificate keys, or cached credentials — any of which enable direct authentication to the appliance or downstream systems without needing passwords. Step 6: A second vulnerability in the /wsfed/passive endpoint (WS-Federation passive authentication) follows the same pattern and can be chained for additional memory regions, providing attackers with redundant exploitation vectors.
what_you_should_do: Immediately verify if your NetScaler instances are configured as SAML IDP by checking for 'add authentication samlIdPProfile' in the running configuration — if present, patch to 14.1-66.59 or 13.1-62.23 on an emergency basis

Apply Citrix's patches immediately: upgrade to NetScaler ADC/Gateway 14.1-66.59, 13.1-62.23, or 13.1-37.262 (FIPS/NDcPP); for versions 14.1-60.52 and 14.1-60.57, Citrix has also released Global Deny List signatures as a temporary mitigation

If patching is delayed, use a Web Application Firewall or reverse proxy to block all external requests to /saml/login and /wsfed/passive endpoints that do not originate from known SAML service providers
Review NetScaler access logs for exploitation attempts: look for POST or GET requests to /saml/login containing SAMLRequest parameters that lack the ACS URL, particularly from external IPs
Rotate any credentials, certificates, or API keys that may have been processed by the NetScaler appliance since March 23, 2026, as memory disclosure may have exposed them before patching
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: Citrix NetScaler has been one of the most targeted network edge devices over the past three years, with previous critical vulnerabilities (Citrix Bleed, CVE-2023-4966) causing widespread enterprise compromises. Attackers are well-practiced in rapidly operationalizing NetScaler exploits before patch adoption is complete. This vulnerability's use of a cookie to return leaked memory is particularly elegant from an attacker's perspective — it requires no special tooling and the response is directly readable from standard HTTP responses. The WS-Federation endpoint providing a second independent exploitation path means even organizations that block SAML traffic remain exposed if they use WS-Federation authentication. The data that can be leaked — session tokens, TLS private keys, credentials in memory — can each independently lead to full appliance compromise and lateral movement into the internal network.

tactics.description: Attackers probe /cgi/GetAuthMethods on NetScaler appliances to enumerate enabled authentication flows and identify SAML IDP configurations before attempting CVE-2026-3055 exploitation
Attacker sends crafted SAMLRequest to /saml/login or /wsfed/passive endpoint on internet-facing NetScaler appliance to trigger out-of-bounds memory read and receive leaked memory in NSC_TASS cookie
Memory regions leaked via NSC_TASS cookie may contain TLS private keys, session tokens, or cached credentials from the NetScaler appliance's active memory
Administrator session tokens present in appliance memory can be extracted from leaked memory contents, enabling session hijacking without credential theft
Session tokens or private keys extracted from memory leaks enable direct authentication to the NetScaler management interface or downstream internal applications protected by the SAML IDP

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying reconnaissance and exploitation attempts targeting the SAML IDP endpoints. Key indicators include abnormal traffic to /saml/login and /wsfed/passive from external sources, specifically requests missing required SAML fields, and probes to /cgi/GetAuthMethods which attackers use to confirm SAML IDP is enabled before exploitation. Organizations should also monitor for the NSC_TASS cookie appearing in response traffic to external requestors from unexpected IP ranges.

log_sources.description: Monitor for HTTP requests to /saml/login and /wsfed/passive from external IPs, particularly POST requests with SAMLRequest parameters; look for requests missing AssertionConsumerServiceURL field indicating exploitation attempts

log_sources.source: Web Application Firewall / Reverse Proxy Logs
log_sources.description: Monitor for requests to /cgi/GetAuthMethods from external IPs (reconnaissance indicator) and any outbound responses containing the NSC_TASS cookie to non-standard SAML service provider IPs
Review kernel and daemon logs for memory access violations or anomalous process behavior in the SAML processing daemon following unusual inbound SAML request patterns

Deploy signatures to detect SAMLRequest payloads missing AssertionConsumerServiceURL field targeting /saml/login; Citrix Global Deny List signatures are available for 14.1-60.52 and 14.1-60.57 as a temporary WAF-style mitigation

what_to_look_for.indicator: HTTP requests to /cgi/GetAuthMethods from external IPs
what_to_look_for.description: Attackers probe this endpoint to confirm SAML IDP is enabled before exploiting CVE-2026-3055; external reconnaissance probes to this endpoint indicate active targeting of the appliance
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.path='/cgi/GetAuthMethods' AND src_ip NOT IN internal_ranges

what_to_look_for.indicator: POST/GET to /saml/login with SAMLRequest lacking ACS URL
what_to_look_for.description: The exploitation payload omits the AssertionConsumerServiceURL field; any SAML authentication request from an unexpected source IP that lacks this field is a high-fidelity exploitation indicator
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.path='/saml/login' AND http.request contains 'SAMLRequest' AND http.request NOT contains 'AssertionConsumerServiceURL'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Outbound HTTP responses containing NSC_TASS cookie to non-SAML SP IPs
what_to_look_for.description: The NSC_TASS cookie appearing in responses to IPs that are not registered SAML Service Providers indicates successful memory disclosure to an attacker
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.response contains 'NSC_TASS' AND dst_ip NOT IN known_saml_sp_ips

what_to_look_for.indicator: POST requests to /wsfed/passive from unexpected source IPs
what_to_look_for.description: watchTowr confirmed a second exploitation vector via the WS-Federation passive endpoint; external requests to this endpoint that don't match known WS-Federation service providers indicate scanning or exploitation
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.path='/wsfed/passive' AND src_ip NOT IN known_wsfed_sp_ips

what_to_look_for.indicator: NetScaler SAML IDP configuration present (add authentication samlIdPProfile)
what_to_look_for.description: The presence of this configuration directive confirms the device is in the vulnerable configuration; devices without SAML IDP configuration are NOT affected by CVE-2026-3055

immediate_actions.action: Check if SAML IDP is configured and patch immediately
immediate_actions.description: Run the following command on the NetScaler CLI to check for SAML IDP configuration; if output is returned, the device is in the vulnerable configuration and must be patched urgently

Apply patches: NetScaler ADC/Gateway 14.1-66.59, 13.1-62.23, or 13.1-37.262 (FIPS/NDcPP). For systems on 14.1-60.52 or 14.1-60.57, apply Citrix Global Deny List signatures as a temporary mitigation while scheduling a full upgrade

immediate_actions.action: Block /saml/login and /wsfed/passive from external untrusted IPs
immediate_actions.description: If immediate patching is not possible, use NetScaler responder policies or a perimeter WAF to block external access to SAML IDP endpoints; only allow known SAML Service Provider IP addresses

immediate_actions.action: Review access logs for exploitation indicators
immediate_actions.description: Search NetScaler access logs since March 23, 2026 for requests to /saml/login lacking AssertionConsumerServiceURL, requests to /cgi/GetAuthMethods from external IPs, and unusual traffic to /wsfed/passive

immediate_actions.action: Rotate TLS certificates and session credentials
immediate_actions.description: If exploitation cannot be ruled out since March 23, rotate TLS private keys for all certificates installed on the NetScaler, revoke and reissue certificates, and force re-authentication for all active administrator sessions
